subSlides.text: On Monday, October 19, 1987, the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell five hundred eight points — twenty-two and a half percent — in a single session. It remains the largest single-day percentage decline in the history of the American stock market. The crash was global: markets in Hong Kong, London, and Frankfurt fell simultaneously.

The proximate causes were multiple and contested. US trade deficit data released the previous week was worse than expected. Treasury Secretary James Baker threatened currency intervention, raising fears about the dollar. Interest rates had been rising. The market was expensive by historical standards.

But the mechanism of the crash was amplified by portfolio insurance — a strategy, developed by academics Hayne Leland and Mark Rubinstein, designed to protect institutional portfolios from losses. Portfolio insurance worked by selling stock index futures as prices fell. When markets declined, it generated selling. That selling caused further declines. The hedge became the cause.

The futures market and the stock market disconnected. Futures traded at a deep discount to underlying stocks, because there were no buyers. Arbitrageurs who would normally close such gaps could not: their computers could not execute trades fast enough, and their credit lines were being cut. Market structure itself failed.

By the end of trading, one trillion dollars in market value had been erased. The financial system did not collapse. Within two years, stock markets had recovered their pre-crash levels. But the day revealed how interconnected, fragile, and poorly understood modern financial markets had become.

Black Monday exposed three structural problems in financial markets. First: the illusion of liquidity. Portfolio insurance assumed that futures markets would remain liquid enough to execute large sell orders without moving prices. Under stress, this assumption failed catastrophically. Strategies designed to be individually safe were collectively destabilizing.

Second: the feedback loop between program trading and prices. Electronic trading systems executing pre-programmed sell orders as prices fell created a mechanical spiral. The market had no circuit breakers, no mechanism to pause and allow human judgment to intervene.

Third: the disconnect between derivative markets and underlying securities. When the futures market diverged sharply from the cash market, arbitrage — the mechanism meant to keep them aligned — broke down. The market structure was not resilient to extreme stress.

The Federal Reserve, under Alan Greenspan who had taken office just two months earlier, faced a critical test. If banks withdrew credit from securities dealers and brokers, the market might not open on Tuesday. The financial system's plumbing was at risk of seizing.

Before markets opened on Tuesday, October 20th, the Federal Reserve issued a one-sentence statement: 'The Federal Reserve, consistent with its responsibilities as the nation's central bank, affirmed today its readiness to serve as a source of liquidity to support the economic and financial system.' This signaled clearly that the Fed would provide whatever liquidity was needed.

The Fed then put the words into action, conducting open market operations to inject reserves into the banking system and encouraging banks to continue lending to securities firms. The intervention was targeted and effective. Markets opened Tuesday. The panic did not spread to the banking system.

The Brady Commission report, issued in January 1988, identified portfolio insurance and the disconnect between futures and cash markets as the principal technical causes of the crash. It recommended circuit breakers — automatic trading halts — to allow markets to pause during extreme moves.

Circuit breakers were implemented. The Chicago Mercantile Exchange and the New York Stock Exchange coordinated trading halts for extreme price movements. Margin requirements were adjusted. Coordination between exchanges was formalized. The market structure was hardened against the specific failure mode of 1987.

The economic impact of Black Monday was surprisingly limited. The feared recession did not materialize. Consumer confidence dipped but recovered. The real economy grew in 1988. The crash was catastrophic for stock prices and negligible for employment and output — unusual in financial crisis history.

The reason was the Fed's rapid and credible intervention. By assuring liquidity and encouraging banks to maintain credit lines, the Fed prevented the stock market crash from becoming a credit crisis. The transmission mechanism from financial markets to the real economy was blocked.

Black Monday established what came to be called the 'Greenspan put' — the expectation that the Federal Reserve would intervene to stabilize financial markets in a crisis. This expectation created moral hazard: if the Fed would cushion market downturns, risk-taking was subsidized. The implications would compound over the following two decades.

The market's recovery also established a template for financial crisis response: central bank liquidity provision, followed by structural reform of market infrastructure. This template would be deployed repeatedly, with diminishing effect, in subsequent crises. Each intervention solved the immediate problem while creating the conditions for the next.

Greenspan's willingness to act — and markets' expectation that he would — was built on the credibility that Volcker had reconstructed after the stagflation crisis (Episode 6). The Greenspan put was only credible because the Volcker precedent had proven the Fed could act decisively when the stakes were existential.
